question: [File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setMarketSettlementFee] Can the feeSetter call setMarketSettlementFee(id, index, newFee) for index=0 (0d breakpoint) to set the post-maturity settlement fee to maximum (0.14 bps cap), under the precondition that the market has already matured and post-maturity takes use the 0d breakpoint, causing post-maturity unwinding trades to pay maximum fees, violating the invariant that post-maturity trading purpose is to facilitate unwinding when liquidations are unprofitable, causing scoped impact: fee overclaim during post-maturity settlement that discourages unwinding and leaves bad debt unresolved? Proof idea: warp past maturity, set 0d fee to max, execute post-maturity take, assert fee is applied and compare to pre-change fee.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / take (liquidation lock bypass)] Can an authorized operator call take(offer, ratifierData, units, victim, receiver, takerCallback, takerCallbackData) where victim is the seller and is currently liquidation-locked (liquidationLocked(id, victim) == true), under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true and the liquidation lock is set during a callback, causing the take to proceed despite the lock because the lock check is only for liquidate not for take, violating the invariant that liquidations are locked for the seller during the callbacks of take, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption where victim's debt increases while they are mid-liquidation? Proof idea: nested take during onBuy callback with victim as seller; assert SellerIsLiquidatable() or liquidation lock prevents the nested take.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setDefaultSettlementFee / setMarketSettlementFee] Can the feeSetter call setDefaultSettlementFee(loanToken, index, newFee) and setMarketSettlementFee(id, index, newFee) in the same transaction via multicall, under the precondition that the market for loanToken was just created in the same multicall batch (touchMarket called first), causing the market to inherit the default fee and then immediately have it overridden, violating the invariant that market config is immutable after creation (fees are mutable by feeSetter but the sequence matters), causing scoped impact: fee manipulation where market is created with one fee and immediately changed before any taker can observe the initial fee? Proof idea: multicall([touchMarket, setDefaultSettlementFee, setMarketSettlementFee]); assert final fee is the market-specific override.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: claimContinuousFee] Can the feeClaimer call claimContinuousFee(market, amount, receiver) where amount > withdrawable but amount <= continuousFeeCredit, under the precondition that withdrawable < continuousFeeCredit due to prior bad debt realizations reducing withdrawable, causing withdrawable to underflow (Solidity 0.8 reverts via UtilsLib.toUint128 overflow check), or if the subtraction is unchecked, causing withdrawable to wrap around to a large value, violating the invariant that contract token balances must cover withdrawable assets, causing scoped impact: protocol insolvency where withdrawable becomes corrupted? Proof idea: unit test claimContinuousFee with amount > withdrawable; assert revert or assert withdrawable remains >= 0.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / take (group consumption)] Can an authorized operator call setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim) to cancel all of victim's offers in a group, then call setConsumed(group, 0, victim) which should revert with AlreadyConsumed(), under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true, violating the invariant that consumed state can only increase, causing scoped impact: if AlreadyConsumed check is bypassable, offer replay enabling double-fill? Proof idea: unit test setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim) then setConsumed(group, 0, victim); assert second call reverts with AlreadyConsumed().

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setMarketTickSpacing / setMarketSettlementFee] Can the tickSpacingSetter and feeSetter (potentially the same address) atomically via multicall call setMarketTickSpacing(id, 1) and setMarketSettlementFee(id, 0, 0) to simultaneously open all ticks and zero fees, under the precondition that both roles are held by the same address, causing a market where all ticks are accessible and no fees are charged, violating the invariant that protocol fees must be correctly attributed, causing scoped impact: fee underclaim where protocol earns nothing from trades in the manipulated market? Proof idea: multicall([setMarketTickSpacing(id,1), setMarketSettlementFee(id,0,0)]); execute take; assert claimableSettlementFee unchanged.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / withdraw (pendingFee rounding)] Can an authorized operator call withdraw(market, 1, victim, attacker) repeatedly in a loop, under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true and victim has large credit with pendingFee > 0, causing each withdrawal of 1 unit to compute pendingFeeDecrease = pendingFee.mulDivUp(1, credit) which rounds up, causing victim's pendingFee to decrease faster than proportional to credit withdrawn, violating the invariant that pendingFee updates are rounded in favor of the user, causing scoped impact: fee accounting corruption where victim's pendingFee is drained faster than expected? Proof idea: fuzz withdrawal amounts from 1 to credit; assert sum(pendingFeeDecrease) <= original pendingFee.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setRoleSetter / setFeeSetter / setFeeClaimer / setTickSpacingSetter] Can the roleSetter call all four role-setting functions in a single multicall to simultaneously transfer all roles to attacker-controlled addresses, under the precondition that roleSetter is a compromised EOA and multicall is used for atomicity, causing all protocol administrative functions to be controlled by the attacker in a single transaction, violating the invariant that protocol roles must be managed with appropriate access controls, causing scoped impact: complete takeover of fee configuration, fee claiming, and tick spacing management? Proof idea: multicall([setRoleSetter(a), setFeeSetter(b), setFeeClaimer(c), setTickSpacingSetter(d)]); assert all roles updated atomically.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / liquidate (liquidator gate bypass)] Can an authorized operator call liquidate(market, collateralIndex, seizedAssets, 0, victim, false, attacker, address(0), '') where market.liquidatorGate restricts liquidators, under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true but liquidate does not check isAuthorized for the liquidator (msg.sender), causing the liquidatorGate check to apply to msg.sender (attacker) not to the authorized operator, violating the invariant that liquidator gate must not be bypassed through alternate liquidation or callback paths, causing scoped impact: unauthorized liquidation by a gated-out liquidator? Proof idea: deploy liquidatorGate that blocks attacker; assert liquidate reverts with LiquidatorGatedFromLiquidating() even if attacker is authorized by victim.
